news_summary: Recent scientific advancements reveal breakthroughs in brain mapping, reversible male contraception, a diet drastically reducing cholera infections, and promising methods to reverse cellular aging.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées scientifiques ont permis des percées dans la cartographie du cerveau, la contraception masculine réversible, un régime alimentaire réduisant considérablement les infections au choléra et des méthodes prometteuses pour inverser le vieillissement cellulaire.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances científicos revelan descubrimientos revolucionarios en el campo de la cartografía cerebral, la anticoncepción masculina reversible, una dieta que reduce drásticamente las infecciones por cólera y métodos prometedores para revertir el envejecimiento celular.

articles.title: Scientists map the brain’s hidden wiring using RNA barcodes in major breakthrough
articles.summary: Researchers have developed a cutting-edge technique that uses RNA “barcodes” to map how neurons connect, capturing thousands of links with single-synapse precision. The method transforms brain mapping into a sequencing task, making it faster and more scalable than traditional approaches. In mice, it revealed surprising new connections between brain cells that were previously unknown. This could open the door to earlier detection and targeted treatment of neurological diseases.
articles.content:
Researchers have developed a powerful new way to map how brain cells connect by tagging neurons with molecular "barcodes." Using this technique, they were able to chart thousands of neural connections in the mouse brain with remarkable speed and detail.

This method could deepen understanding of how complex brain networks are organized and how they function. It may also shed light on what goes wrong in neurological disorders and how diseases like Alzheimer's develop over time.

"When engineering a computer, you need to know the circuitry of the central processing unit. If you don't know how everything is wired together, you can't understand its function, optimize it or fix it when something breaks. We are approaching the brain the same way," said study leader Boxuan Zhao, a professor of cell and developmental biology at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign.

"Our technology enables simultaneous mapping of thousands of neural connections with single-synapse resolution -- a capability that doesn't exist in any current technology. It is directly applicable to understanding circuit dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and could provide a platform for developing circuit-guided therapeutic interventions," he said.

The findings were published in the journal Nature Methods.

A Faster, More Detailed Way To Map the Brain

Mapping the brain has traditionally been slow and difficult. Scientists often had to slice brain tissue into extremely thin sections, image them with microscopes and piece together the pathways manually. While newer sequencing-based tools can label many neurons at once, they usually show where a neuron extends rather than identifying the exact cells it connects with at the synapse, Zhao said.

To overcome this limitation, Zhao's team created a new platform called Connectome-seq. It assigns each neuron a unique RNA "barcode." Specialized proteins carry these barcodes from the neuron's main body to the synapse, the point where two neurons meet.

Researchers then isolate these synapses and use high-throughput sequencing to read which barcode pairs are found together. This reveals which neurons are directly connected, allowing scientists to map networks on a large scale.

Turning Brain Wiring Into a Sequencing Problem

"We translated the neural connectivity problem into a sequencing problem. Imagine a big bunch of balloons. The main body of each balloon has its unique barcode stickers all over it, and some move down to the end of the string. If two balloons are tied together at the end, the two barcodes meet at the junction," Zhao said. "Then we snip out the knots and sequence the barcodes in each one. If the same knot has stickers from balloon A and balloon B, we know these two balloons are tied together. We are doing this in the brain, just on the level of thousands of neuron cells. With this information, we can reconstruct a sophisticated map that represents the connections among all these seemingly floaty groups."

Discovering New Brain Circuit Connections

Using Connectome-seq, the team mapped more than 1,000 neurons in a mouse brain circuit known as the pontocerebellar circuit, which links two brain regions. The analysis revealed previously unknown patterns of connectivity, including direct links between cell types that had not been known to connect in the adult brain.

"With improvements already underway in our lab, we are confident that we can make it even better and eventually reach the goal of mapping the whole mouse brain," Zhao said.

Potential To Transform Alzheimer's and Brain Disease Research

Because it is both fast and scalable, Connectome-seq could significantly accelerate research into neurodegenerative diseases, psychiatric conditions and other brain disorders. By comparing brain connections in healthy individuals with those at different stages of disease, scientists may be able to identify early changes in neural circuits.

"With sequencing-based approaches, the time and cost are greatly reduced, which really makes it possible to see differences in different brains. We could see where connections change, where the most vulnerable parts of the brain are, perhaps before symptoms even appear," Zhao said. "For example, if we can catch where exactly the weak link is that kick starts the whole catastrophic cascade in Alzheimer's disease, can we specifically strengthen those connections to where the disease slows or does not progress?"

The research was supported by a Neuro-omics Initiative grant from Wu Tsai Neurosciences Institute of Stanford University, as well as funding from the Elsa U. Pardee Foundation and the Edward Mallinckrodt Jr. Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in brain mapping using RNA barcodes, which enables faster, more detailed understanding of neural connections. This advancement has broad implications for understanding and potentially treating neurological diseases like Alzheimer's, benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context about the method, its advantages over previous techniques, and its potential impact on health research.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create faster brain map using RNA barcodes to reveal hidden connections
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Understanding how brain cells, called neurons, connect is very important for learning how the brain works and what happens in brain diseases. But mapping these connections has been slow and difficult until now.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Illinois developed a new technique called Connectome-seq. It uses special RNA “barcodes” to tag neurons so scientists can see exactly which cells connect at tiny junctions called synapses. They tested this method on mouse brains, mapping over 1,000 neurons and discovering new connections that were not known before.

**Impact** – This new way of mapping brain connections is faster and more detailed than older methods. It turns the complex problem of brain wiring into a sequencing task, making it easier to study large brain networks. This could help scientists understand how diseases like Alzheimer’s change brain connections early on, possibly leading to better treatments.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to improve Connectome-seq to map the entire mouse brain. They also hope to use this method to compare healthy and diseased brains, which could reveal where brain problems start and how to stop them.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new RNA barcode method lets scientists quickly map brain cell connections, opening doors to better understanding and treating brain diseases.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une carte cérébrale plus rapide grâce aux codes-barres ARN révélant des connexions cachées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Comprendre comment les cellules du cerveau, appelées neurones, se connectent est essentiel pour savoir comment fonctionne le cerveau et ce qui se passe dans les maladies cérébrales. Mais cartographier ces connexions était lent et difficile jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de l’Illinois ont développé une nouvelle technique appelée Connectome-seq. Elle utilise des codes-barres ARN spéciaux pour marquer les neurones afin que les scientifiques puissent voir exactement quelles cellules sont connectées aux synapses, les petites jonctions. Ils ont testé cette méthode sur des cerveaux de souris, cartographiant plus de 1 000 neurones et découvrant de nouvelles connexions inconnues auparavant.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle façon de cartographier les connexions cérébrales est plus rapide et plus précise que les méthodes anciennes. Elle transforme le problème complexe du câblage du cerveau en une tâche de séquençage, facilitant l’étude des grands réseaux cérébraux. Cela pourrait aider à comprendre comment des maladies comme Alzheimer modifient les connexions cérébrales très tôt, ce qui pourrait mener à de meilleurs traitements.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe cherche à améliorer Connectome-seq pour cartographier tout le cerveau de la souris. Ils espèrent aussi utiliser cette méthode pour comparer les cerveaux sains et malades, afin de découvrir où commencent les problèmes cérébraux et comment les arrêter.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode utilisant des codes-barres ARN permet aux scientifiques de cartographier rapidement les connexions des cellules cérébrales, ouvrant la voie à une meilleure compréhension et traitement des maladies du cerveau.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean un mapa cerebral más rápido con códigos de barras de ARN para revelar conexiones ocultas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Entender cómo se conectan las células del cerebro, llamadas neuronas, es clave para saber cómo funciona el cerebro y qué sucede en las enfermedades cerebrales. Pero mapear estas conexiones ha sido lento y difícil hasta ahora.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Illinois desarrollaron una nueva técnica llamada Connectome-seq. Usa códigos de barras de ARN especiales para marcar las neuronas y así ver exactamente qué células se conectan en las sinapsis, las pequeñas uniones. Probaron este método en cerebros de ratones, mapeando más de 1,000 neuronas y encontrando conexiones nuevas que no se conocían.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva forma de mapear las conexiones cerebrales es más rápida y detallada que los métodos anteriores. Convierte el complicado problema del cableado cerebral en una tarea de secuenciación, facilitando el estudio de grandes redes cerebrales. Esto podría ayudar a entender cómo enfermedades como el Alzheimer cambian las conexiones cerebrales temprano, lo que podría llevar a mejores tratamientos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea mejorar Connectome-seq para mapear todo el cerebro del ratón. También esperan usar este método para comparar cerebros sanos y enfermos, para descubrir dónde comienzan los problemas cerebrales y cómo detenerlos.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo método con códigos de barras de ARN permite a los científicos mapear rápido las conexiones de células cerebrales, abriendo camino a mejores estudios y tratamientos de enfermedades cerebrales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a network of glowing, intertwined neuron-like branches, each tipped with unique colorful molecular "barcode" patterns, connected by delicate synapse-like knots resembling tied ribbons, set against a soft, natural background symbolizing a mouse brain circuit.

articles.title: Scientists discover reversible male birth control that stops sperm production
articles.summary: Scientists at Cornell University may be closing in on the long-sought “holy grail” of male contraception: a safe, reversible, nonhormonal method that completely halts sperm production. In a breakthrough mouse study, researchers used a compound called JQ1 to temporarily shut down meiosis—the critical process that produces sperm—without causing lasting harm. After treatment stopped, sperm production bounced back, fertility returned, and the animals produced healthy offspring.
articles.content:
Cornell University scientists have taken a major step toward developing a safe, reversible, long-acting and 100% effective nonhormonal male contraceptive, considered the holy grail of male contraception.

In a proof-of-principle study conducted in mice over six years, the team showed that interrupting a key step in meiosis, the process that produces sex cells, can temporarily halt sperm production without causing lasting harm.

The findings were published today (April 7) in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

To achieve this, scientists used JQ1, a small molecule inhibitor originally developed to study cancer and inflammatory diseases. While JQ1 is not suitable as a treatment due to neurological side effects, it is known to interfere with a stage of meiosis called prophase 1. This allowed researchers to demonstrate, for the first time, that targeting meiosis can safely and reversibly shut down sperm production.

"We're practically the only the group that's pushing the idea that contraception targets in the testis are a feasible way to stop sperm production," said Paula Cohen, professor of genetics and director of the Cornell Reproductive Sciences Center.

"Our study shows that mostly we recover normal meiosis and complete sperm function, and more importantly, that the offspring are completely normal," Cohen said.

Why New Male Birth Control Options Are Needed

Current male contraceptive options remain limited to condoms and vasectomies. While vasectomies are considered long-term, many men hesitate to undergo the procedure, even though reversal surgery is sometimes possible. At the same time, researchers have been cautious about developing hormonal approaches, partly due to safety concerns observed in women.

Cohen and her team focused on meiosis rather than other stages of sperm development to ensure that sperm production could be fully stopped while still allowing it to recover later. This approach also preserves overall reproductive health.

"We didn't want to impact the spermatogonial stem cells, because if you kill those, a man will never become fertile again," Cohen said. Also, once sperm entered spermiogenesis, there was a potential for viable sperm to leak out and fertilize an egg.

How JQ1 Temporarily Disrupts Fertility

JQ1 works by disrupting meiosis during prophase 1, causing developing cells to die at that stage. It also blocks the gene activity required for later stages of sperm development.

In the study, male mice received JQ1 for three weeks. During this time, sperm production stopped completely, and key features of meiosis, including chromosome behavior during prophase 1, were disrupted.

Once the treatment ended, recovery began. Within six weeks, most normal meiotic processes returned, along with healthy sperm production. The researchers then bred the mice and confirmed that they were fertile. Their offspring were also healthy and able to reproduce.

"It shows that we recover complete meiosis, complete sperm function, and more importantly, that the offspring are completely normal," Cohen said.

What a Future Male Contraceptive Could Look Like

If developed for human use, this type of male contraceptive could be delivered as an injection given every three months or possibly as a patch to maintain effectiveness, Cohen said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in developing a safe, reversible, and nonhormonal male contraceptive that temporarily halts sperm production without lasting harm. This advancement addresses a major gap in male contraception options, has broad societal impact by expanding family planning choices, and is supported by detailed research findings.
articles.personality_title: Scientists develop reversible male birth control that stops sperm production
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Male contraception options today are mostly limited to condoms and vasectomies. Vasectomies are often permanent, and hormonal methods for men have faced safety concerns. Scientists have been searching for a safe, reversible, nonhormonal method to temporarily stop sperm production without lasting effects.

**What happened** – Researchers at Cornell University studied a compound called JQ1 in mice. They found that JQ1 temporarily stops sperm production by interrupting a process called meiosis, which is how sperm cells develop. After treatment ended, sperm production and fertility returned to normal, and the mice had healthy offspring.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have shown that it is possible to safely and fully stop sperm production without harming the ability to have children later. The method targets a specific stage in sperm development, avoiding damage to stem cells that produce sperm. This discovery could lead to a new kind of male birth control that is effective and reversible.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to develop this approach into a form men could use, like an injection every three months or a patch. More studies will be needed to make sure it is safe and effective for humans.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found a way to safely and temporarily stop sperm production in mice, opening the door to new reversible male birth control methods.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques développent une contraception masculine réversible qui bloque la production de spermatozoïdes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aujourd’hui, les options de contraception masculine se limitent surtout au préservatif et à la vasectomie. La vasectomie est souvent permanente, et les méthodes hormonales pour les hommes posent des problèmes de sécurité. Les scientifiques cherchent une méthode sûre, réversible et non hormonale pour arrêter temporairement la production de spermatozoïdes sans effets durables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’université Cornell ont étudié un composé appelé JQ1 chez la souris. Ils ont découvert que JQ1 arrête temporairement la production de spermatozoïdes en interrompant un processus appelé méiose, qui produit les cellules sexuelles. Après l’arrêt du traitement, la production de spermatozoïdes et la fertilité sont revenues à la normale, et les souris ont eu une descendance en bonne santé.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques montrent qu’il est possible d’arrêter complètement la production de spermatozoïdes sans nuire à la capacité d’avoir des enfants plus tard. La méthode cible une étape précise du développement des spermatozoïdes, évitant ainsi d’endommager les cellules souches. Cette découverte pourrait mener à un nouveau type de contraception masculine efficace et réversible.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent développer cette méthode sous forme d’injection tous les trois mois ou de patch. D’autres études seront nécessaires pour garantir la sécurité et l’efficacité chez l’humain.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont réussi à arrêter temporairement et en toute sécurité la production de spermatozoïdes chez la souris, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles méthodes de contraception masculine réversibles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos desarrollan un anticonceptivo masculino reversible que detiene la producción de esperma
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las opciones actuales de anticoncepción masculina son principalmente condones y vasectomías. La vasectomía suele ser permanente, y los métodos hormonales para hombres han generado preocupaciones de seguridad. Los científicos buscan un método seguro, reversible y no hormonal para detener temporalmente la producción de esperma sin causar daño permanente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cornell estudiaron un compuesto llamado JQ1 en ratones. Descubrieron que JQ1 detiene temporalmente la producción de esperma al interrumpir un proceso llamado meiosis, que es cómo se desarrollan las células espermáticas. Después de terminar el tratamiento, la producción de esperma y la fertilidad regresaron a la normalidad, y los ratones tuvieron crías saludables.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se demuestra que es posible detener completamente la producción de esperma sin dañar la capacidad de tener hijos después. El método actúa en una etapa específica del desarrollo del esperma, evitando dañar las células madre. Este avance podría dar lugar a un nuevo tipo de anticonceptivo masculino efectivo y reversible.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan desarrollar esta técnica en una forma que los hombres puedan usar, como una inyección cada tres meses o un parche. Se necesitan más estudios para asegurar su seguridad y eficacia en humanos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos han encontrado una forma segura y temporal de detener la producción de esperma en ratones, abriendo el camino para nuevos métodos reversibles de anticoncepción masculina.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized, semi-abstract test tube gently cradling a delicate, glowing double helix intertwined with tiny, suspended sperm cells that fade softly into translucent, blooming flowers symbolizing fertility and renewal, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: This diet could slash cholera infections by up to 100x
articles.summary: A surprising new study reveals that what you eat could play a powerful role in fighting cholera, a deadly diarrheal disease. Researchers found that diets rich in certain proteins—especially casein from dairy and wheat gluten—can dramatically reduce the ability of cholera bacteria to take hold in the gut, in some cases cutting infection levels by up to 100 times. These proteins appear to disable a key “weapon” the bacteria use to attack other microbes and dominate the gut environment.
articles.content:
Cholera, a dangerous bacterial infection that causes severe diarrhea and can be fatal without treatment, may be significantly reduced through diet. New research from the University of California, Riverside suggests that eating more protein could help limit the infection's ability to take hold in the body.

The study found that diets rich in casein, the primary protein found in milk and cheese, along with wheat gluten, greatly reduced how much cholera bacteria could colonize the gut.

"I wasn't surprised that diet could affect the health of someone infected with the bacteria. But the magnitude of the effect surprised me," said Ansel Hsiao, UCR associate professor of microbiology and plant pathology and senior author of the study published in Cell Host and Microbe.

"We saw up to 100-fold differences in the amount of cholera colonization as a function of diet alone," Hsiao said.

Testing Different Diets on Cholera Infection

Scientists have long known that diet shapes the community of microbes living in the gut. In this study, researchers wanted to see whether harmful, disease-causing bacteria would also respond to dietary changes.

To test this, they fed infected mice different diets. Some were high in protein, others high in simple carbohydrates, and others high in fat. The results were striking. High-fat diets had little impact on the infection, and carbohydrate-heavy diets offered only modest benefits. In contrast, diets rich in dairy proteins and wheat gluten almost completely prevented the bacteria from establishing itself in the gut.

"The high-protein diet had one of the strongest anti-cholera effects compared to a balanced diet. And not all proteins are the same," Hsiao said. "Casein and wheat gluten were the two clear winners."

How Protein Disarms Cholera Bacteria

Further investigation revealed why these proteins were so effective. They interfere with a tiny, syringe-like structure on the surface of cholera bacteria that is used to inject toxins into nearby cells. This structure, known as the type 6 secretion system, or T6SS, helps the bacteria compete with other microbes and dominate the gut.

When this system is suppressed, cholera struggles to kill competing bacteria and cannot easily take over the intestinal environment.

A Potential Low-Cost Alternative to Antibiotics

Cholera remains a serious public health concern in regions with limited access to clean water, particularly in parts of Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa. Standard treatment focuses on rehydration, while antibiotics can shorten the illness but do not eliminate the toxins left behind.

There is also concern about the overuse of antibiotics, which can lead to drug-resistant bacteria. While antibiotic-resistant cholera is not currently widespread, bacteria can adapt quickly, and treatments may become less effective over time.

"Dietary strategies won't generate antibiotic resistance in the same way a drug might," Hsiao said.

Because of this, dietary approaches could provide a safer and more affordable way to reduce the severity or likelihood of infection, especially in vulnerable populations.

"Wheat gluten and casein are recognized as safe in a way a microbe is not, in a regulatory sense, so this is an easier way to protect public health," Hsiao said.

What This Means for Humans

Although the findings are based on mouse studies, researchers believe similar effects could occur in humans. Hsiao plans to explore how these dietary changes influence the human gut microbiome and whether they could also help defend against other infectious bacteria.

"Some diets will be more successful than others, but if you try this for pathogens other than cholera, I suspect we'll also see a beneficial effect," Hsiao said. "The more we can improve peoples' diets, the more we may be able to protect people from succumbing to disease."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that certain dietary proteins can dramatically reduce cholera infection, a major global health threat. This finding offers a potentially low-cost, accessible, and antibiotic-free method to combat a deadly disease affecting vulnerable populations worldwide, with broad public health implications and detailed scientific context.
articles.personality_title: Certain proteins in diet may cut cholera infections up to 100 times
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cholera is a dangerous bacterial disease that causes severe diarrhea and can be deadly if untreated. It mainly affects people in regions without clean water, and current treatments rely on rehydration and antibiotics.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of California, Riverside studied how diet affects cholera infections in mice. They found that diets high in specific proteins, especially casein from dairy and wheat gluten, drastically reduced the amount of cholera bacteria in the gut—sometimes by as much as 100 times. These proteins block a key tool the bacteria use to attack other microbes and take over the gut.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a simple, food-based way to fight cholera without relying on antibiotics. Since overusing antibiotics can cause bacteria to become resistant, a dietary method could be safer and easier to use, especially in poor communities. The proteins tested are safe and common in many diets, making this approach accessible.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study if the same effects happen in humans and whether these diets can help protect against other harmful bacteria. This could lead to new, low-cost ways to reduce infections using food.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating proteins like casein and wheat gluten may greatly reduce cholera infections by weakening the bacteria's ability to attack the gut environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Certaines protéines alimentaires pourraient réduire les infections à choléra jusqu’à 100 fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le choléra est une maladie bactérienne dangereuse qui cause de fortes diarrhées et peut être mortelle sans traitement. Elle touche surtout les populations des régions où l’eau potable manque. Les traitements actuels reposent sur la réhydratation et les antibiotiques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à Riverside ont étudié l’effet de l’alimentation sur les infections à choléra chez la souris. Ils ont découvert que des régimes riches en certaines protéines, surtout la caséine du lait et le gluten de blé, réduisent fortement la quantité de bactéries dans l’intestin, jusqu’à 100 fois moins. Ces protéines bloquent un outil clé que la bactérie utilise pour attaquer d’autres microbes et dominer l’intestin.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre qu’un simple changement alimentaire peut aider à lutter contre le choléra sans utiliser d’antibiotiques. Or, l’usage excessif d’antibiotiques peut rendre les bactéries résistantes. Une méthode basée sur l’alimentation pourrait donc être plus sûre et accessible, notamment dans les pays pauvres. Ces protéines sont courantes et reconnues comme sûres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont vérifier si les mêmes effets se produisent chez l’humain et si ces régimes peuvent aussi protéger contre d’autres bactéries nuisibles. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à des moyens peu coûteux de réduire les infections grâce à la nourriture.

**En une phrase** – Manger des protéines comme la caséine et le gluten de blé pourrait fortement diminuer les infections à choléra en affaiblissant la capacité des bactéries à attaquer l’intestin.
articles.personality_title_es: Ciertas proteínas en la dieta podrían reducir infecciones de cólera hasta 100 veces
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cólera es una enfermedad bacteriana peligrosa que causa diarrea grave y puede ser mortal si no se trata. Afecta principalmente a personas en zonas sin agua limpia, y los tratamientos actuales usan rehidratación y antibióticos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de California en Riverside estudiaron cómo la dieta afecta las infecciones de cólera en ratones. Descubrieron que dietas ricas en proteínas específicas, especialmente caseína de lácteos y gluten de trigo, reducen mucho la cantidad de bacterias en el intestino, hasta 100 veces menos. Estas proteínas bloquean una herramienta clave que la bacteria usa para atacar a otros microbios y dominar el intestino.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra una forma sencilla de combatir el cólera sin depender de antibióticos. El uso excesivo de antibióticos puede causar resistencia bacteriana, por lo que un método basado en la dieta podría ser más seguro y fácil de usar, especialmente en comunidades pobres. Las proteínas estudiadas son seguras y comunes en muchas dietas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar si estos efectos también ocurren en humanos y si estas dietas pueden ayudar a proteger contra otras bacterias dañinas. Esto podría llevar a nuevas formas económicas de reducir infecciones usando alimentos.

**Frase resumen** – Comer proteínas como caseína y gluten de trigo puede reducir mucho las infecciones de cólera al debilitar la capacidad de las bacterias para atacar el intestino.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized gut environment portrayed as a peaceful village where strong golden strands of wheat gluten and creamy white casein proteins form protective walls around small, defeated blue and green syringe-shaped bacteria, symbolizing cholera, with soft natural earth tones and gentle light emphasizing harmony and health.

articles.title: This method to reverse cellular ageing is about to be tested in humans
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 07 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01024-7A burgeoning field is launching its first clinical trial to find out whether dialling back cell development can safely refresh aged tissues and organs.
articles.content:
Yuancheng Ryan Lu could barely breathe while he waited for his labmate to adjust the microscope focus.

On the slide in front of them were the results of Lu’s latest attempt to turn back time for ageing retinal nerve cells. If it worked, the method he was using could help to restore eyesight to older adults with glaucoma, an age-related condition that damages the optic nerve. And perhaps some day it could be used to rejuvenate organs such as the kidneys or liver — maybe even the brain.

Longevity is in the genes: half of lifespan is heritable

Lu had spent three years trying different approaches — and had failed. But this time looked different. Lu had introduced three genes into mouse eyes that should revert cells to a younger developmental state. And there under the microscope he thought he could see signs of new growth. Now, he was asking his labmate to confirm his suspicions. “I was so nervous,” says Lu, now a geneticist at the Whitehead Institute in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

When the verdict was in, Lu remembers jumping up and down and high-fiving his colleagues in the microscope room. Yet, he couldn’t help but worry that the celebration might be short-lived.

Lu and his colleagues were among several teams trying to ‘partially reprogram’ cells to a younger state. Now, seven busy years later, his discovery1 is the basis for a clinical trial set to start this year. It will be a pivotal test of a burgeoning field that has attracted researchers in academia and industry — as well as billions of dollars of private investment and the attention of Silicon Valley’s tech elite. The trial will attempt to answer an evocative question: can old cells safely be made young again?

The answer, some say, could reshape the very concept of ageing. It could provide a way to rejuvenate old organs — or, in its most extreme and optimistic formulation, the entire human body. Partial reprogramming also promises to write a new chapter for the foundational discovery, 20 years ago, that adult cells can be reprogrammed to an embryonic-stem-cell-like state2.

But risks loom just as large as the promises: push a cell too close to that stem-like state and it could lose its ability to function properly, and even become cancerous. “When cells lose their identity, we know that comes with some forms of danger,” says Tamir Chandra, who studies ageing at the Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minnesota.

Rejuvenation factors

In 2006, Shinya Yamanaka, a stem-cell biologist then at Kyoto University in Japan, and his colleague discovered that four proteins known as transcription factors — later dubbed Yamanaka factors — could transform an adult cell into an induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cell that is capable of taking on new identities2. The finding was hailed as breakthrough that could pave the way to stem-cell based therapies in which iPS cells are coaxed into adopting a certain fate and then injected into a patient. In February, regulators in Japan endorsed the approval of the first such iPS-cell-based therapies — for severe heart failure and Parkinson’s disease.

But some researchers wondered whether the Yamanaka factors might be put to another use. In 2010, Prim Singh, a chromatin biologist now at Nazarbayev University in Astana, Kazakhstan, and his colleague Fred Zacouto proposed that researchers could introduce the genes that encode the factors briefly, but then turn them off before cells become completely reset (see ‘Turning back cellular time’). Then, they suggested, the cells might become younger without losing their identity3.

It was a difficult idea for some researchers to accept, Singh says: at the time, most were focused on exploring iPS cells, not rejuvenation.

In 2016, another publication pushed the nascent field into the limelight. Juan Carlos Izpisúa Belmonte, a stem-cell biologist then at the Salk Institute for Biological Studies in La Jolla, California, and his colleagues reported that they had temporarily and repeatedly turned the Yamanaka factors on and off in mice4. This cyclic expression extended the lifespan of model animals with a condition called progeria, which causes accelerated ageing. In normal, old mice, the factors improved regeneration of damaged muscle and pancreatic tissue.

The next few years were a boon for partial reprogramming efforts in mice. Scientists applied Yamanaka factors to rejuvenate skin cells and reduce scar tissue5, to boost muscle regeneration6 and to allow the heart cells to regenerate after injury7, to name just a few examples. One study even suggested that cyclic expression of the Yamanaka factors in the brains of aged mice improved their performance on memory tests8.

Different groups experimented with ways to make the Yamanaka factors safe. Some researchers cycled genes on and off, others turned them on only transiently, in the hope that they would not be active long enough to fully reprogram the cells. Although the approaches seemed to be safe in mice, doubts lingered about leaving cells with unknown potential in the body. “I’d argue that a dinosaur is not a good pet, even if you trained it very well,” says Daniel Ives, chief executive of Shift Bioscience in Cambridge, UK.

How your brain controls ageing — and why zombie cells could be key

Lu and others decided to remove one of the factors, the protein c-Myc, high levels of which can cause cancer. In one attention-grabbing study9, researchers introduced the three remaining factors into cells throughout the bodies of old mice. “We injected the mice and expected them to die, to be honest,” says Noah Davidsohn, lead author on the study and chief scientific officer at Rejuvenate Bio, an ageing-focused biotechnology company in San Diego, California.

But months ticked by, and no tumours formed. Instead, several measures of health improved, and the mice lived longer than their unreprogrammed counterparts. It was a preliminary study, but others have also found that the three Yamanaka factors can be used in mice safely, says Vittorio Sebastiano, a stem-cell and reproductive biologist at the University of California, Irvine. Even so, he worries that leaving out c-Myc could have drawbacks; the protein’s other functions, such as aiding cell division, might be important for some partially reprogrammed cells.

For now, the field is showing enough promise to draw the eye of some of the technology industry’s wealthiest investors. In 2020, a select group of researchers gathered in Los Altos Hills, California, to discuss the future of partial reprogramming with Internet entrepreneur Yuri Milner. “There was a lot of excitement,” says Vadim Gladyshev, a researcher who studies ageing at Harvard Medical School in Boston, Massachusetts, and who attended the meeting. “There was the feeling of something big.”

Record investment

The meeting led to the founding, with Izpisúa Belmonte, of Altos Labs, a reprogramming-focused company that launched in 2022 with US$3 billion from Milner and other investors. It set a world record for biotech start-up financing. That show of support popped the cork for investment, and other Silicon Valley backers entered the fray. Altos Labs “was like a giant X-marks-the-spot”, says Ives. “Now all of a sudden you had a lot of investors that wanted exposure on this opportunity.”

How quickly are you ageing? What molecular ‘clocks’ can tell you about your health

Sam Altman, the chief executive of Open AI in San Francisco, California, invested in a longevity company called Retro Biosciences in Redwood City, California, which is working on partial reprogramming, among other projects. Brian Armstrong, the chief executive of the cryptocurrency exchange Coinbase, helped found a partial-reprogramming company called NewLimit in South San Francisco, California.

But it is Life Biosciences, a biotechnology company in Boston, Massachusetts, that will probably be the first to test partial reprogramming in people. The company was co-founded by Lu’s PhD adviser, David Sinclair, who studies ageing at Harvard Medical School and has been criticized by other researchers for making bold claims about purported anti-ageing treatments. Life Biosciences aims to build on Sinclair and Lu’s work by using a virus to shuttle three Yamanaka factors, without c-Myc, into one eye in people who have retinal nerve damage because of glaucoma.

The company will proceed slowly, says Sharon Rosenzweig-Lipson, chief scientific officer at the company, treating up to 12 people with a specific type of glaucoma, and then up to 6 people with another condition, called NAION, that causes acute optic nerve damage. The genes will be regulated by a genetic switch that turns them on only when participants take a certain antibiotic. Studies in monkeys have found no evidence of cancer or other harmful effects from the procedure, Rosenzweig-Lipson says, and participants will be followed up for at least five years.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a groundbreaking clinical trial testing a method to reverse cellular aging in humans, which could rejuvenate aged tissues and organs, potentially transforming treatment for age-related diseases and improving quality of life on a broad scale. It provides detailed context on the scientific background, previous animal studies, risks, and the significance of this first human trial, highlighting a major breakthrough in health and longevity research.
articles.personality_title: First human trial begins to test method reversing cell aging
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how to reverse aging at the cellular level to restore damaged tissues. A key discovery in 2006 showed that certain proteins, called Yamanaka factors, can turn adult cells back into stem-like cells. Since then, researchers have explored using these factors briefly to make cells younger without losing their identity. Animal studies showed this method can improve tissue repair and extend life in mice.

**What happened** – In 2026, a company called Life Biosciences is starting the first clinical trial in humans to test partial cell reprogramming. They will use three Yamanaka factors (excluding one linked to cancer) delivered by a virus into the eyes of people with glaucoma, a disease that damages the optic nerve. The genes can be switched on and off with an antibiotic to control the treatment. The trial will include a small number of patients and carefully monitor safety and effects.

**Impact** – This trial is the first time partial reprogramming will be tested in people. If successful, it could lead to new treatments that refresh aging cells and organs, improving vision and potentially other age-related conditions. It builds on years of animal research and large investments from scientists and tech leaders, showing strong interest in slowing or reversing aging.

**What's next step** – The trial will track patients for at least five years to watch for benefits and any risks, like cancer. If the method proves safe and effective, it could expand to treat other tissues and diseases. Researchers will also continue improving how to control and apply the reprogramming factors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new clinical trial is testing a promising method to rewind cell aging in humans, aiming to restore damaged tissues safely for the first time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Premier essai humain pour tester une méthode de rajeunissement cellulaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à inverser le vieillissement au niveau cellulaire pour réparer les tissus endommagés. Une découverte clé en 2006 a montré que certaines protéines, appelées facteurs Yamanaka, peuvent transformer des cellules adultes en cellules souches. Depuis, les chercheurs explorent l’utilisation temporaire de ces facteurs pour rajeunir les cellules sans qu’elles perdent leur identité. Des études sur des animaux ont montré que cette méthode améliore la réparation des tissus et prolonge la vie des souris.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, une entreprise nommée Life Biosciences lance le premier essai clinique chez l’homme pour tester ce rajeunissement partiel des cellules. Trois facteurs Yamanaka (sans celui lié au cancer) seront introduits par un virus dans les yeux de personnes atteintes de glaucome, une maladie qui endommage le nerf optique. Les gènes pourront être activés ou désactivés grâce à un antibiotique pour contrôler le traitement. L’essai inclura un petit nombre de patients et surveillera attentivement la sécurité et les effets.

**Impact** – Cet essai est la première fois que le rajeunissement partiel est testé chez l’humain. S’il réussit, il pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouveaux traitements qui rafraîchissent les cellules et organes vieillissants, améliorant la vue et d’autres maladies liées à l’âge. Cela s’appuie sur des années de recherches animales et d’importants investissements de scientifiques et de leaders technologiques.

**Prochaine étape** – L’essai suivra les patients pendant au moins cinq ans pour observer les bénéfices et les risques, comme le cancer. Si la méthode est sûre et efficace, elle pourrait être étendue à d’autres tissus et maladies. Les chercheurs continueront aussi d’améliorer le contrôle et l’application des facteurs de reprogrammation.

**Une phrase clé** – Un nouvel essai clinique teste une méthode prometteuse pour inverser le vieillissement cellulaire chez l’humain, visant à restaurer en toute sécurité les tissus endommagés.
articles.personality_title_es: Primer ensayo humano para probar método que revierte el envejecimiento celular
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo revertir el envejecimiento a nivel celular para reparar tejidos dañados. Un descubrimiento clave en 2006 mostró que ciertas proteínas, llamadas factores Yamanaka, pueden convertir células adultas en células madre. Desde entonces, los investigadores exploran usar estos factores brevemente para rejuvenecer las células sin que pierdan su identidad. Estudios en animales mostraron que este método mejora la reparación de tejidos y extiende la vida en ratones.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, una empresa llamada Life Biosciences inicia el primer ensayo clínico en humanos para probar la reprogramación parcial de células. Usarán tres factores Yamanaka (excluyendo uno vinculado al cáncer) introducidos por un virus en los ojos de personas con glaucoma, una enfermedad que daña el nervio óptico. Los genes pueden activarse y desactivarse con un antibiótico para controlar el tratamiento. El ensayo incluirá pocos pacientes y supervisará cuidadosamente la seguridad y efectos.

**Impacto** – Este ensayo es la primera vez que se prueba la reprogramación parcial en personas. Si tiene éxito, podría abrir nuevos tratamientos para rejuvenecer células y órganos envejecidos, mejorando la visión y otras enfermedades relacionadas con la edad. Se basa en años de investigaciones en animales y grandes inversiones de científicos y líderes tecnológicos.

**Próximo paso** – El ensayo seguirá a los pacientes por al menos cinco años para observar beneficios y riesgos, como el cáncer. Si el método es seguro y efectivo, podría extenderse a otros tejidos y enfermedades. Los investigadores también mejorarán cómo controlar y aplicar los factores de reprogramación.

**Una frase clave** – Un nuevo ensayo clínico prueba un método prometedor para revertir el envejecimiento celular en humanos, con el objetivo de restaurar tejidos dañados de forma segura por primera vez.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate, glowing eye-shaped seed sprouting vibrant, youthful nerve fibers intertwined with gentle strands of DNA helixes, set against a soft, warm background of fading autumn leaves transforming into fresh green shoots, symbolizing the revival and partial reprogramming of aging retinal cells through genetic rejuvenation.
